volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Children of Heracles (Section 165 - Section 330)

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: θάψεις νεκροὺς πεσόντας ; ἦ κακὸν λόγον
sections.translation: will they have fallen whom thou buriest ? Surely thou wilt get an evil name from the citizens , if for the sake of an old man with one foot in the grave , a mere shadow I may say , and for these children , thou wilt plunge into troublous waters . The best [*] thou canst say is , that thou wilt find in them a hope , and nothing more ;

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: καὶ τοῦτο πολλῷ τοῦ παρόντος ἐνδεές ·
sections.translation: and yet this falls far short of the present need ; for these would be but a poor match for Argives even when fully armed and in their prime , if haply that raises thy spirits ; moreover , the time ’ twixt now and then is long , wherein ye may be blotted out . Nay , hearken to me ;

sections.label: Section 175
sections.original: δοὺς μηδέν , ἀλλὰ τἄμ ’ ἐῶν ἄγειν ἐμὲ
sections.translation: give me naught , but let me take mine own , and so gain Mycenae ; but forbear to act now , as is your Athenian way , and take the weaker side , when it is in thy power to choose the stronger as thy friends .

sections.label: Section 179
sections.original: τίς ἂν δίκην κρίνειεν ἢ γνοίη λόγον ,
sections.translation: Who can decide a cause or ascertain its merits ,

sections.label: Section 180
sections.original: πρὶν ἂν παρʼ ἀμφοῖν μῦθον ἐκμάθῃ σαφῶς ;
sections.translation: till from both sides he clearly learn what they would say ?

sections.label: Section 181
sections.original: ἄναξ — ὑπάρχει μὲν τόδʼ ἐν τῇ σῇ χθονί —
sections.translation: O king , in thy land I start with this advantage , the right to hear and speak in turn , and none , ere that , will drive me hence as elsewhere they would . ’ Twixt us and him is naught in common ,

sections.label: Section 185
sections.original: ἐπεὶ γὰρ Ἄργους οὐ μέτεσθʼ ἡμῖν ἔτι ,
sections.translation: for we no longer have aught to do with Argos since that decree was passed , but we are exiles from our native land ; how then can he justly drag us back as subjects of Mycenae , [*] seeing that they have banished us ? For we are strangers .

sections.label: Section 190
sections.original: φεύγειν δικαιοῦθʼ ὅστις ἂν τἄργος φύγῃ ;
sections.translation: Or do ye claim that every exile from Argos is exiled from the bounds of Hellas ? Not from Athens surely ; for ne ’ er will she for fear of Argos drive the children of Heracles from her land . Here is no Trachis , not at all ; no ! nor that Achaean town , whence thou , defying justice ,

sections.label: Section 195
sections.original: τὸ δʼ Ἄργος ὀγκῶν , οἷάπερ καὶ νῦν λέγεις ,
sections.translation: but boasting of the might of Argos in the very words thou now art using , didst drive the suppliants from their station at the altar . If this shall be , and they thy words approve , why then I trow this is no more Athens , the home of freedom . Nay , but I know the temper and nature of these citizens ;

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: θνῄσκειν θελήσουσʼ · ἡ γὰρ αἰσχύνη πάρος
sections.translation: they would rather die , for honour ranks before mere life with men of worth . Enough of Athens ! for excessive praise is apt to breed disgust ; and oft ere now I have myself felt vexed at praise that knows no bounds .

sections.label: Section 205
sections.original: σοὶ δʼ ὡς ἀνάγκη τούσδε βούλομαι φράσαι
sections.translation: But to thee , as ruler of this land , I fain would show the reason why thou art bound to save these children . Pittheus was the son of Pelops ; from him sprung Aethra , and from her Theseus thy sire was born . And now will I trace back these children ’ s lineage for thee .

sections.label: Section 210
sections.original: Ἡρακλέης ἦν Ζηνὸς Ἀλκμήνης τε παῖς ,
sections.translation: Heracles was son of Zeus and Alcmena ; Alcmena sprang from Pelops ’ daughter ; therefore thy father and their father would be the sons of first cousins . Thus then art thou to them related , O Demophon , but thy just debt to them beyond the ties of kinship

sections.label: Section 215
sections.original: τεῖσαι λέγω σοι παισί · φημὶ γάρ ποτε
sections.translation: do I now declare to thee ; for I assert , in days gone by , I was with Theseus on the ship , as their father ’ s squire , when they went to fetch that girdle fraught with death ; yea , and from Hades ’ murky dungeons did Heracles bring thy father up ; as all Hellas doth attest .

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: ὧν ἀντιδοῦναί σʼ οἵδʼ ἀπαιτοῦσιν χάριν ,
sections.translation: [*] Wherefore in return they crave this boon of thee , that they be not surrendered up nor torn by force from the altars of thy gods and cast forth from the land . For this were shame on thee , and [*] hurtful likewise in thy state , should suppliants , exiles ,

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: βλέψον πρὸς αὐτοὺς βλέψον · — ἕλκεσθαι βίᾳ .
sections.translation: kith and kin of thine , be haled away by force . For pity ’ s sake ! cast one glance at them . I do entreat thee , laying my suppliant bough upon thee , by thy hands and beard , slight not the sons of Heracles , now that thou hast them in thy power to help . Show thyself their kinsman and their friend ;

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: πατὴρ ἀδελφὸς δεσπότης · ἅπαντα γὰρ
sections.translation: be to them father , brother , lord ; for better each and all of these than to fall beneath the Argives ’ hand .

sections.label: Section 232
sections.original: ᾤκτιρʼ ἀκούσας τούσδε συμφορᾶς , ἄναξ .
sections.translation: O king , I pity them , hearing their sad lot Now more than ever do I see noble birth o ’ ercome by fortune ; for these , though sprung from a noble sire ,

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: ἐσθλοῦ γεγῶτες δυστυχοῦσʼ ἀναξίως .
sections.translation: are suffering what they ne ’ er deserved .

sections.label: Section 236
sections.original: τρισσαί μʼ ἀναγκάζουσι συμφορᾶς ὁδοί ,
sections.translation: Three aspects of the case constrain me , Iolaus , not to spurn the guests thou bringest ; first and foremost , there is Zeus , at whose altar thou art seated with these tender children gathered round thee ;

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: τὸ συγγενές τε καὶ τὸ προυφείλειν καλῶς
sections.translation: next come ties of kin , and the debt I owe to treat them kindly for their father ’ s sake ; and last , mine honour , which before all I must regard ; for if I permit this altar to be violently despoiled by stranger hands , men will think

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: οἰκεῖν δοκήσω γαῖαν , Ἀργείων δʼ ὄκνῳ
sections.translation: the land I inhabit is free no more , and that through fear [*] I have surrendered suppliants to Argives , and this comes nigh to make one hang oneself . Would that thou hadst come under a luckier star ! yet , as it is , fear not that any man shall tear thee and these children from the altar by force .

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: σὺ δʼ Ἄργος ἐλθὼν ταῦτά τʼ Εὐρυσθεῖ φράσον ,
sections.translation: Get thee ( to Copreus ) to Argos and tell Eurystheus so ; yea and more , if he have any charge against these strangers , he shall have justice ; but never shalt thou drag them hence .

sections.label: Section 253
sections.original: οὐκ ἢν δίκαιον ᾖ τι καὶ νικῶ λόγῳ ;
sections.translation: Not even if I have right upon my side and prove my case ?

sections.label: Section 254
sections.original: καὶ πῶς δίκαιον τὸν ἱκέτην ἄγειν βίᾳ ;
sections.translation: How can it be right to drag the suppliant away by force ?

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: οὐκ οὖν ἐμοὶ τόδʼ αἰσχρόν , ἀλλʼ οὐ σοὶ βλάβος ;
sections.translation: Well , [*] mine is the disgrace ; no harm will come to thee .

sections.label: Section 256
sections.original: ἐμοί γʼ , ἐάν σοι τούσδʼ ἐφέλκεσθαι μεθῶ .
sections.translation: Tis harm to me , if I let them be haled away by thee .

sections.label: Section 257
sections.original: σὺ δʼ ἐξόριζε , κᾆτʼ ἐκεῖθεν ἄξομεν .
sections.translation: Banish them thyself , and then will I take them from elsewhere .

sections.label: Section 258
sections.original: σκαιὸς πέφυκας τοῦ θεοῦ πλείω φρονῶν .
sections.translation: Nature made thee a fool , to think thou knowest better than the god .

sections.label: Section 259
sections.original: δεῦρʼ , ὡς ἔοικε , τοῖς κακοῖσι φευκτέον .
sections.translation: It seems then evildoers are to find a refuge here .

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: ἅπασι κοινὸν ῥῦμα δαιμόνων ἕδρα .
sections.translation: A temple of the gods is an asylum open to the world .

sections.label: Section 261
sections.original: ταῦτʼ οὐ δοκήσει τοῖς Μυκηναίοις ἴσως .
sections.translation: Maybe they will not take this view in Mycensa .

sections.label: Section 262
sections.original: οὐκ οὖν ἐγὼ τῶν ἐνθάδʼ εἰμὶ κύριος ;
sections.translation: What ! am I not lord of this domain ?

sections.label: Section 263
sections.original: βλάπτων γʼ ἐκείνους μηδέν , ἢν σὺ σωφρονῇς .
sections.translation: So long as thou injure not the Argives , and if [*] wise , thou wilt not .

sections.label: Section 264
sections.original: βλάπτεσθʼ , ἐμοῦ γε μὴ μιαίνοντος θεούς .
sections.translation: Be injured for all I care , provided I sin not against the gods .

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: οὐ βούλομαί σε πόλεμον Ἀργείοις ἔχειν .
sections.translation: I would not have thee come to blows with Argos .

sections.label: Section 266
sections.original: κἀγὼ τοιοῦτος · τῶνδε δʼ οὐ μεθήσομαι .
sections.translation: I am of like mind in this ; but I will not dismiss these from my protection .

sections.label: Section 267
sections.original: ἄξω γε μέντοι τοὺς ἐμοὺς ἐγὼ λαβών .
sections.translation: For all that , I shall take and drag my own away .

sections.label: Section 268
sections.original: οὐκ ἆρ ’ ἐς Ἄργος ῥᾳδίως ἄπει πάλιν .
sections.translation: Why then perhaps thou wilt find a difficulty in returning to Argos .

sections.label: Section 269
sections.original: πειρώμενος δὴ τοῦτό γʼ αὐτίκʼ εἴσομαι .
sections.translation: That shall I soon find out by making the attempt .

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: κλαίων ἄρʼ ἅψῃ τῶνδε κοὐκ ἐς ἀμβολάς .
sections.translation: Touch them and thou shalt rue it , and that without delay .

sections.label: Section 271
sections.original: μὴ πρὸς θεῶν κήρυκα τολμήσῃς θενεῖν .
sections.translation: I conjure thee , never dare to strike a herald .

sections.label: Section 272
sections.original: εἰ μή γʼ ὁ κῆρυξ σωφρονεῖν μαθήσεται .
sections.translation: Strike I will , unless that herald learn discretion .

sections.label: Section 273
sections.original: ἄπελθε · καὶ σὺ τοῦδε μὴ θίγῃς , ἄναξ .
sections.translation: Depart ; and thou , O king , touch him not .

sections.label: Section 274
sections.original: στείχω · μιᾶς γὰρ χειρὸς ἀσθενὴς μάχη .
sections.translation: I go ; for ’ tis feeble fighting with a single arm .

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: ἥξω δὲ πολλὴν Ἄρεος Ἀργείου λαβὼν
sections.translation: But I will come again , bringing hither a host of Argive troops , spearmen clad in bronze ; for countless warriors are awaiting my return , and king Eurystheus in person at their head ; anxiously he waits the issue here on the borders of Alcathous ’ [*] realm .

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: λαμπρὸς δʼ ἀκούσας σὴν ὕβριν φανήσεται
sections.translation: And when he hears thy haughty answer , he will burst upon thee , and thy citizens , on this land and all that grows therein ; for all in vain should we possess such hosts of picked young troops in Argos , should we forbear to punish thee . [ Exit Copreus .

sections.label: Section 284
sections.original: φθείρου · τὸ σὸν γὰρ Ἄργος οὐ δέδοικʼ ἐγώ .
sections.translation: Perdition seize thee ! I am not afraid of thy Argos .

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: ἐνθένδε δʼ οὐκ ἔμελλες αἰσχύνας ἐμὲ
sections.translation: Be very sure thou shalt not drag these suppliants hence by force , to my shame ; for I hold not this city subject unto Argos , but independently .

sections.label: Section 288
sections.original: ὥρα προνοεῖν , πρὶν ὅροις πελάσαι
sections.translation: ’ Tis time to use our forethought , ere the host of Argos approach our frontier ,

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: μάλα δʼ ὀξὺς Ἄρης ὁ Μυκηναίων ,
sections.translation: for exceeding fierce are the warriors of Mycenae , and in the present case still more than heretofore . For all heralds observe this custom , to exaggerate what happened twofold . Bethink thee what a tale he will tell his master

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: ὡς δείνʼ ἔπαθεν καὶ παρὰ μικρὸν
sections.translation: of his dreadful treatment , how he came very near losing his life altogether .

sections.label: Section 297
sections.original: οὐκ ἔστι τοῦδε παισὶ κάλλιον γέρας ,
sections.translation: Children have no fairer prize than this , the being born of a good and noble sire , and the power to wed from noble families ; but whoso is enslaved by passion

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: κακοῖς ἐκοινώνησεν , οὐκ ἐπαινέσω ,
sections.translation: and makes a lowborn match , I cannot praise for leaving to his children a legacy of shame , to gratify himself . For noble birth offers a stouter resistance to adversity than base parentage ; we , for instance , in the last extremity of woe , have found friends

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: καὶ ξυγγενεῖς τούσδʼ , οἳ τοσῆσδʼ οἰκουμένης
sections.translation: and kinsmen here , the only champions of these children through all the length and breadth of this Hellenic world . Give , children , give to them your hand , and they the same to you ; draw near to them . Ah ! children , we have made trial of our friends ,

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: ἢν δʼ οὖν ποθʼ ὑμῖν νόστος ἐς πάτραν φανῇ
sections.translation: and if ever ye see the path that leads you back to your native land , and possess your home and the honours of your father , count them ever as your friends and saviours , and never lift against their land the foeman ’ s spear , in memory of this , but hold this city

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: πασῶν νομίζετʼ . ἄξιοι δʼ ὑμῖν σέβειν
sections.translation: first midst those ye love . Yea , they well deserve your warm regard , in that they have shifted from our shoulders to their own the enmity of so mighty a land as Argos and its people , though they saw we were vagabonds and beggars ; still they did not give us up nor drive us forth .

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ καὶ ζῶν καὶ θανών , ὅταν θάνω ,
sections.translation: So while I live , and after death ,— come when it will ,— loudly will I sing thy praise , good friend , and will extol thee as I stand at Theseus ’ side , and cheer his heart , as I tell how thou didst give kind welcome and protection to the sons of Heracles , and how nobly

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: σῴζεις πατρῴαν δόξαν , ἐξ ἐσθλῶν δὲ φὺς
sections.translation: thou dost preserve thy father ’ s fame through the length of Hellas , and hast not fallen from the high estate , to which thy father brought thee , a lot which few others can boast ; for ’ mongst the many wilt thou find one maybe , that is not degenerate from his sire .

sections.label: Section 329
sections.original: ἀεί ποθʼ ἥδε γαῖα τοῖς ἀμηχάνοις
sections.translation: This land is ever ready

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: σὺν τῷ δικαίῳ βούλεται προσωφελεῖν .
sections.translation: in an honest cause to aid the helpless . Wherefore ere now it hath endured troubles numberless for friends , and now in this I see a struggle nigh at hand .
